lesson:
The kanji 行 started life as a picture of a crossroads — two paths meeting. In the oldest oracle bone inscriptions, it looked like an intersection viewed from above. It didn't originally mean "to go." It meant the road itself. The meaning drifted over centuries from the place where you walk to the act of walking on it.

That's why 行 lives this double life in modern Japanese: as いく it's the verb "to go," but in compounds it often carries its older sense of a path or institution — 銀行 (bank, a "silver firm"), 旅行 (travel, a "journey-going"), 行列 (a procession, people moving along a path).

For the sound: いく is two sharp syllables — i-ku — that feel like they're already moving somewhere. Say it out loud and notice how your mouth opens with い and then snaps shut with く, like a little departure. Now say くる (to come). It starts closed and rounds inward. いく pushes out. くる pulls in. Keep those two as a pair and the sounds start to feel like they mean what they mean.

Watch out: 行く's て-form is いって, not the いいて you'd expect. It's the only く-verb in the language that does this. Here's why: 行く used to be ゆく (and you'll still hear ゆく in songs and formal speech). The って form is a fossil of that older pronunciation — ゆく → ゆって, which smoothed into いって even after the main verb shifted to いく. The irregularity isn't random. It's a piece of the word's history that didn't get cleaned up.

Particles: 行く takes に or へ — both mark the destination, the place you're heading toward. They're nearly interchangeable, but に is more precise ("to that specific spot") while へ is more about the general direction ("toward"). In practice, に is far more common. You'll also see を行く occasionally — that marks the path itself: この道を行く (go along this road).

Passive に行かれる: the person who does the going is marked with に. Passive with 行く often carries a nuance of being negatively affected — 彼に先に行かれた (he went ahead of me — and I was left behind). The に marks who did it to you.

Causative を／に行かせる: you're making or letting someone go. Use を when you're forcing it — 子供を行かせる (make the kid go). Use に when you're permitting it — 子供に行かせる (let the kid go). The distinction is subtle and not always strict, but を leans coercive and に leans permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞

Cond ～ばいけばに／へ

The kanji 見 is ancient. In oracle bone script — the earliest Chinese writing, carved into tortoise shells over 3,000 years ago — it was a drawing of a human figure with an enormous eye for a head. Not a person with an eye. A person who is an eye. The whole body existed to carry that eye around. What survived into the modern kanji is 目 (the eye) sitting on top of 儿 (the legs that carry it). Every time you write 見, you're drawing a little creature whose entire purpose is to look.

Mnemonic: mi-ru → mirror. The English word comes from Latin mirari, "to look at, to wonder at" — the same root as "miracle" and "admire." Japanese and Latin aren't related, so this is pure coincidence — but coincidences make great anchors. みる, mirror. You look in a mirror to 見る yourself.

見る does the work of three English verbs. テレビを見る (watch TV). 景色を見る (look at the scenery). 夢を見る (see a dream) — yes, in Japanese you see dreams, you don't have them. The word doesn't care about the distinction between passive seeing and active looking. Context handles it.

Key pattern: 〜てみる. Attach みる to any verb's て-form and it means "try doing it to see what happens." 食べてみる (try eating it), 聞いてみる (try asking). You do the action in order to see the result. Once you notice this pattern, you'll hear it everywhere.

Particles: 見る takes を because you're acting on something — directing your sight at it. The thing being seen is the direct object: 映画を見る, テレビを見る.

Potential が見られる: を shifts to が because in potential form, the thing you can see becomes the subject — it's not something you're doing to it, it's something that's possible. 富士山が見られる (Mt. Fuji can be seen).

Passive に見られる: the person who sees you is marked with に. 彼に見られた (I was seen by him). You're the one being looked at — に marks who did the looking.

Causative に／を見させる: you're making someone see something. The person you're making look takes に, and the thing they look at takes を: 子供に映画を見させる (make the kid watch the movie). に marks the person, を marks the thing.

Grammar trap: You will, at some point, try to say 多い人 — "many people" — and it will be wrong. Not grammatically wrong in a way anyone can cleanly explain, just wrong. Japanese doesn't let 多い sit in front of a noun like other い-adjectives. 大きい犬 (big dog) is fine. 多い人 isn't. You have to say 多くの人 instead, switching 多い into its adverbial form to make it work. Nobody really knows why this adjective refuses to behave. Its opposite 少ない (few) does the same thing. They're the only two.

Where 多い does work is at the end of a sentence, after が: 人が多い. 最近、雨が多い. 日本は祝日が多い. It describes a situation — "people are numerous," "rain is plentiful" — rather than pointing at a specific noun. Once you get used to that sentence shape, the word stops fighting you.

The kanji 多 is two 夕 stacked. 夕 is a crescent moon — the earliest versions were literally a drawing of a half-moon. Two of them piled up meant "numerous," probably from the idea of many nights passing. It's one of the simpler kanji etymologies but it works: you see double, there's a lot of something.

Dictionaryおおい
Negativeおおくない
Pastおおかった
Past negおおくなかった
Adverbおおく
て-formおおくて

This kanji has two lives. Read as いえ, it means the physical structure — walls, roof, the building you live in. Read as うち, it means something harder to translate: your home, your household, your people. うちに帰る isn't "return to the building." It's "go back where I belong."

That's because うち shares its sound with 内 — "inside." And inside vs. outside (内 vs 外, うち vs そと) is one of the deepest organizing principles in Japanese social life. Your うち is your in-group: family at home, colleagues at work, classmates at school. うちの会社 means "our company" — not the building, the tribe. うちの子 is "my kid" — claimed, belonging to the inside circle. The word carries warmth and possession at the same time.

The kanji 家 itself: a roof radical (宀) over 豕, which is a pig. A pig under a roof. In ancient China, keeping pigs inside the dwelling was the mark of a settled household — you weren't nomadic anymore, you had a home. The pig isn't random. It's the oldest symbol of domestic permanence.

Japanese has a system that English doesn't: it divides the world into three zones of proximity, not two. Where English has "this" and "that," Japanese has こ (near me), そ (near you), and あ (away from both of us). Add ど for questions. These four prefixes snap onto everything: これ・それ・あれ・どれ for standalone things, ここ・そこ・あそこ・どこ for places, この・その・あの・どの before nouns. One system, totally regular.

これ is the こ member — the thing in my zone. If it's in my hand, on my side, or something I just brought up in conversation, it's これ. The key insight: "my zone" isn't just physical. A topic I introduced, a plan I'm proposing, my own feelings — all これ. これは私の本です (this is my book — holding it). これはどうですか (how about this? — my suggestion).

Sound anchor: こ → "close." The こ words are always the close ones. Keep that and the whole system clicks into place.

If これ is "my zone," それ is "your zone." Not far away (that's あれ) — just on the other person's side. The pen in your hand? それ. The idea you just mentioned? それ. It belongs to your territory in the conversation.

This is the demonstrative English doesn't have. English "that" covers both それ and あれ. Japanese insists on knowing: is it near the listener, or far from everyone? In practice, それ ends up being the most common of the three in conversation, because you're constantly referring back to what the other person said. それはいいですね (that's good — responding to their idea). それは大変 (that's rough — their situation). それ、ちょうだい (give me that — thing near you).

The そ series also pulls double duty as a kind of "the" in storytelling. ある男がいた。その男は… (There was a man. The man…). Once something has been introduced into the shared narrative — it's no longer new, but it's not physically present either — it becomes そ territory. You'll see その and それ constantly in written Japanese for this reason.

The kanji 私 means "private" — as opposed to 公 (おおやけ, public). In classical Chinese, 私 was the individual's own concerns, separate from the affairs of the state. That etymology survives intact: 私立 (しりつ, private — as in a private university), 私用 (しよう, personal business). When you say わたし, you're literally saying "the private one."

Japanese has at least a dozen first-person pronouns, and your choice reveals your gender, age, social position, and mood. わたし is the Switzerland of the bunch — polite enough for a job interview, casual enough for daily life. Men in relaxed settings often switch to 僕 (ぼく, softer, younger-sounding) or 俺 (おれ, rougher, assertive). Women sometimes use あたし, a softened contraction. But わたし works everywhere and offends no one.

The real trick: Japanese drops the subject whenever possible. If it's obvious you're talking about yourself, saying わたし can actually sound stiff or self-important — like starting every English sentence with "I personally." Native speakers leave it out far more often than they say it. Learn the word, then practice not using it.

Two kanji: 仕 (to serve) and 事 (thing, matter). Literally, a "serving-thing" — a thing done in service. The word doesn't split "job" from "work" from "occupation" the way English does. 仕事がある can mean "I have a job," "I have work to do," or "I'm busy right now," all depending on context.

仕事 is a する-noun — it pairs with する directly to become a verb: 仕事する (to work). No extra verb needed. You'll see this pattern everywhere in Japanese — nouns of action that become verbs just by bolting する onto them. 仕事 is one of the most common.

In Japan, 仕事中 (しごとちゅう, "in the middle of work") is practically a force field. It signals that someone is occupied and shouldn't be interrupted without good reason. The compound 仕事が終わる (work ends) marks a real social transition — the person becomes available again. Pay attention to how much conversational space 仕事 occupies in Japanese daily life. It's not just what you do. It's a state of being.

No kanji in modern use — a hint that this word is ancient native Japanese, not a Chinese import. It's a question word that asks "at what point in time?" and it's remarkably flexible in where it can sit in a sentence. いつ来ますか (when will you come?). いつがいい？ (when is good?).

The real power move: Japanese question words shapeshift. Add a particle and いつ becomes a completely different word:

いつも → always ("when-ever-each")
いつか → someday ("when-?" — vague, unspecified)
いつでも → anytime ("when-even")

This isn't special to いつ. Every question word does it: だれも (everyone), なにか (something), どこでも (anywhere). But いつ is a clean one to learn the pattern with, because the meaning shifts are intuitive. Once you see it here, you'll recognize it everywhere.

meaning: to do, to make, to wear accessories
lesson:
する is one of only two irregular verbs in the entire language (the other is 来る). Every other verb in Japanese follows either the ichidan or godan pattern without exception. する makes its own rules — and it's worth memorizing every form, because you'll use this verb more than almost any other.

But する's real significance isn't as a standalone verb. It's a machine that turns nouns into verbs. Take nearly any noun of action and add する: 勉強する (to study), 運動する (to exercise), 旅行する (to travel), 電話する (to phone). Hundreds of verbs work this way. Most are Chinese-origin compounds, but する happily attaches to loanwords too: コピーする, ドライブする. It's the most productive verb-maker in Japanese.

する vs やる: Both mean "to do," but they aren't interchangeable. する is neutral and general. やる is more physical, more hands-on, sometimes rougher — closer to "do" in the sense of "get it done." 何をする？ is "what are you doing?" 何をやる？ is more like "what are you gonna do?" with a sense of action. する also works for decisions (コーヒーにする I'll have coffee) and wearing accessories (ネクタイをする), where やる wouldn't fit.

Particles: As a standalone verb, する takes を — 何をする？ (what will you do?). But when する attaches to a noun to make a する-verb, the particle depends on that noun: 勉強をする (study something), 電話をする (make a call), コーヒーにする (decide on coffee).

Potential ができる: する's potential is the completely separate word できる. It takes が — the thing that's possible is the subject. 日本語ができる (I can do Japanese).

Passive にされる: に marks the agent — the person who did it to you. Often carries a nuance of being negatively affected: ひどいことをされた (something terrible was done to me).

Causative にさせる: に marks the person you make or let do something. 子供に掃除させる (make the kid clean).

The kanji 出 looks like what it means — a thing pushing out of a container. The oldest forms showed a foot stepping beyond a boundary line. In the modern kanji, you can still see it: two layered shapes, one emerging from the other. It's one of the most visually intuitive kanji you'll encounter.

出る covers a surprising range of English verbs. You "leave" a room (部屋を出る), "appear" on TV (テレビに出る), "come out" as a result (答えが出る), and even "attend" a meeting (会議に出る). The common thread: something moves from inside to outside, from hidden to visible, from absent to present.

Pair: 出る is intransitive — things come out on their own. Its transitive partner is 出す (だす) — you put something out, you take something out, you make something come out. 手紙を出す (send a letter — put it out into the world). ゴミを出す (take out the trash). Every time you see 出 in a new compound, ask: is the thing coming out by itself (出る), or is someone making it come out (出す)?

Particles: 出る is intransitive but takes を to mark the place you leave — 部屋を出る (leave the room). This を doesn't mark a direct object — it marks the starting point of movement. Use に to mark where you appear or show up: テレビに出る (appear on TV).

Passive に出られる: indirect passive — someone leaving affects you. 妻に出られた (my wife left me — and I'm the one suffering). に marks the person whose leaving impacted you.

Causative を／に出させる: making or letting someone leave. を leans coercive — 生徒を出させる (make the student leave). に leans permissive — 生徒に出させる (let the student leave).

The kanji 使 has the person radical (亻) on the left and 吏 (a government official) on the right. The original meaning was "to send someone on a mission" — to make a person go do something for you. That's still alive in 大使 (たいし, ambassador — literally "great envoy") and 使者 (ししゃ, messenger). The modern meaning of "to use" is a natural drift: you use a tool the way you'd send a servant — you make it work for you.

つかう is the everyday, all-purpose "use." Money, tools, time, language, your brain — all 使う. お金を使う (spend money). 日本語を使う (use Japanese). 頭を使う (use your head — think hard). The word doesn't distinguish between "use," "spend," and "employ" the way English does.

Sound: tsu-ka-u. The つ gives it a crisp, purposeful start. Notice the う ending — this is a godan verb, and all its conjugations pivot on that final vowel. The stem つか stays rock-solid while the ending shifts.

Particles: 使う takes を — you're acting directly on the thing you use. お金を使う, 頭を使う. The thing is your tool and you're wielding it.

Potential が使える: を shifts to が — what you can use becomes the subject. このカードが使える (this card can be used / is usable).

Passive に使われる: に marks who's doing the using. 部下に道具として使われた (I was used as a tool by my subordinate).

Causative に…を使わせる: に marks the person you make use something, を marks the thing. 子供にパソコンを使わせる (let the kid use the computer).

On the surface, ところ just means "place." いいところ (a nice place). Simple. But ところ is one of those Japanese words that quietly runs half the grammar, and the sooner you notice that, the better.

The trick: ところ doesn't just mean a physical place. It means a point — in space, in time, in a process. When you put a verb in front of it, it becomes a snapshot of where you are in that action:

食べるところ → about to eat (the point just before eating)
食べているところ → in the middle of eating (the point during)
食べたところ → just finished eating (the point right after)

Same word, three completely different time positions, controlled entirely by which form the verb takes. This is ところ's superpower: it pins down exactly where in an action you are. No separate words for "about to," "in the middle of," or "just finished" — ところ handles all three.

The kanji 所 combines a door (戸) and an axe (斤). The original meaning was a place where you cut wood — a workshop. From there it generalized to any place, any spot, any point.

つくる is the broadest "make" verb in Japanese — it covers everything from cooking dinner to writing a novel to building a house. 料理を作る (make food). 計画を作る (make a plan). 友達を作る (make friends). English uses "make," "create," "build," "produce," "craft" — Japanese reaches for 作る for most of these.

Three kanji, one sound: Japanese actually has three different kanji all read つくる, each with a different flavor. 作る is the everyday default — general-purpose making. 造る is for large-scale production: ships, buildings, sake, things that require industrial effort. 創る is for artistic or original creation: a new concept, a work of art, something that didn't exist before. In daily life, 作る covers 95% of situations. The others are refinements you'll see in writing.

The kanji 作 has the person radical on the left (亻) and a component on the right that originally depicted sawing or cutting. A person working with their hands — making something. It's concrete in a way that matches the word's feel: つくる implies hands-on effort, not abstract generation.

Particles: 作る takes を — the thing you make is your direct object. 料理を作る, 計画を作る. You can also use で for the material: 木で椅子を作る (make a chair out of wood).

Potential が作れる: が — the thing that can be made becomes the subject. この材料でケーキが作れる (a cake can be made with these ingredients).

Passive に作られる: に marks the maker. 有名な建築家に作られた (was made by a famous architect).

Causative に…を作らせる: に marks who you make do the making, を marks what they make. 子供にご飯を作らせる (make the kid cook).

The kanji 思 is 田 (field) over 心 (heart). But 田 here isn't actually "field" — it's a stylized picture of the top of a skull, the fontanelle (the soft spot on a baby's head where you can feel the brain). So 思 is literally brain-over-heart: the meeting point of thought and feeling. And that's exactly what 思う means — not cold logical thinking, but thinking-feeling. What you believe, what you sense, what you feel is true.

思う vs 考える: Both translate to "think" but they live in different parts of your head. 思う is opinion, belief, impression — you arrive at it naturally: いいと思う (I think it's good). 考える (かんがえる) is deliberate reasoning — you sit down and work through something: よく考えてください (please think it over carefully). If someone asks your gut reaction, you 思う. If someone asks you to analyze a problem, you 考える.

〜と思う is one of the most-used sentence endings in Japanese. It softens everything. Instead of flatly stating 明日は雨だ ("it'll rain tomorrow"), you say 明日は雨だと思う — "I think it'll rain." Japanese conversation runs on this kind of hedging. Get comfortable appending と思う to almost any statement and you'll sound more natural instantly.

Particles: 思う primarily takes と — the quotative particle that frames what you think. いいと思う (I think it's good). The thought itself goes before と, and 思う closes the sentence. You can also use を when thinking about someone: 彼のことを思う (think of him).

Potential と思える: keeps と — 正しいと思える (it feels like it could be right). 思える adds a nuance of "it strikes me as" rather than "I actively think."

Passive に思われる: に marks who thinks it. みんなに変だと思われている (everyone thinks I'm weird).

Causative に思わせる: に marks the person you make think something. 彼女にそう思わせた (I made her think that).

持つ covers three English verbs at once: to hold (physically), to carry, and to have/possess. 手に持つ (hold in your hand). 荷物を持つ (carry luggage). 車を持っている (own a car). The common thread: something is in your grasp, whether literally or figuratively.

The kanji 持 is 扌 (hand radical) plus 寺 (temple). The temple part isn't about religion here — 寺 was borrowed for its sound value in ancient Chinese. But if you want a visual anchor: a hand holding up a temple. That's a lot of holding power.

The ている form matters: 持つ in its dictionary form means the act of picking up or grabbing. 持っている means the ongoing state of having. This distinction is crucial. 傘を持つ = "grab the umbrella." 傘を持っている = "I have an umbrella (with me)." For possession, you almost always want 持っている.

Watch out: There's a separate word もつ written as 持つ in the vocab list that means "to last, be durable" (no kanji usually). Different word, same sound. Context keeps them apart: この電池はよくもつ (this battery lasts well) is clearly not about holding anything.

Particles: 持つ takes を — the thing you hold, carry, or possess is your direct object. 荷物を持つ (carry luggage). 車を持っている (own a car).

Potential が持てる: が — what you can carry becomes the subject. この重さが持てる？ (can you handle this weight?).

Passive に持たれる: に marks the holder. Uncommon with 持つ but valid: 他人に持たれたくない (I don't want someone else holding it).

Causative に…を持たせる: に marks who you make carry, を marks the thing. 子供に荷物を持たせる (make the kid carry the bags).

The kanji 買 is 网 (a net) over 貝 (a shell). Cowrie shells were the original currency across ancient East Asia — that's why so many money-related kanji contain 貝: 貨 (currency), 財 (wealth), 貯 (savings), 費 (expense). The net over the shell suggests gathering up shells — gathering up money to make a purchase. Once you know that 貝 = money in kanji-world, dozens of characters start making sense.

かう is straightforward: you buy something. 本を買う. 切符を買う. No surprises in the meaning. But pay attention to a subtle idiom: 恨みを買う (to "buy" someone's resentment — to earn their ill will). Even in Japanese, acquiring negative things can be described as "buying" them.

This is an う-ending godan verb, which means its て-form and ない-form do something English speakers might not expect: the う vanishes and gets replaced by って or わない. That わ in 買わない catches people off guard — but it follows the same pattern as all う-verbs (使う → 使わない, 思う → 思わない).

Particles: 買う takes を — the thing you buy is your direct object. 本を買う. You can add で for where you buy: コンビニでお茶を買う. Or に for who you buy it for: 友達にプレゼントを買う.

Potential が買える: が — what you can buy becomes the subject. この値段でビールが買える (beer can be bought at this price).

Passive に買われる: に marks the buyer. あの家は外国人に買われた (that house was bought by a foreigner).

Causative に…を買わせる: に marks who you make buy, を marks the thing. 後輩にお菓子を買わせる (make the junior buy snacks).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryかうを
Negativeかわないを
Politeかいますを
Polite negかいませんを
Pastかったを
Polite pastかいましたを
て-formかってを
Volitionalかおうを
Polite volかいましょうを
Passiveかわれるに
Causativeかわせるに／を
Caus-passかわせられるに
Potentialかえるが
Imperativeかえを
Imp なさいかいなさいを
Cond ～ばかえばを
Cond ～たらかったらを

Two kanji: 時 (time, moment) and 間 (interval, space between). Together: "the space between moments" — duration. This is time you can measure and spend, not time as a philosophical concept.

時間 vs 時: These two get tangled early on. 時間 (じかん) is duration — how long something takes, or the abstract concept of time. 時 (とき) is a moment — "when" something happens. 時間がない means "I don't have time" (duration). 子供の時 means "when I was a child" (a moment/period). Additionally, 時間 doubles as a counter for hours: 三時間 (three hours), while 時 marks clock time: 三時 (three o'clock).

The kanji 間 is worth looking at closely. It's a sun (日) seen through a gate (門) — sunlight streaming through a crack in the door. The original meaning was a gap, a space between things. Time is the gap between events. A room (部屋) is a space between walls. 人間 (にんげん, human being) literally means "person-between" — a being that exists in the space between other people, defined by relationships. This kanji shows up everywhere, and the "gap between" meaning always applies.

The kanji 知 is 矢 (arrow) plus 口 (mouth). An arrow is fast, direct, and hits its target — knowledge that strikes you like an arrow to the mind. It's knowing as a sudden arrival: the moment a fact lands.

知る vs 分かる: Both translate to "know" or "understand," but they're not interchangeable. 知る is about possessing information — a fact is either in your head or it isn't. 分かる (わかる) is about comprehension — something makes sense to you, you grasp it. 彼の名前を知っている (I know his name — the fact is stored). この問題が分かる (I understand this problem — I grasp it). You 知る facts. You 分かる concepts.

Critical: 知る is almost never used in its plain dictionary form in conversation. The act of "coming to know" is instantaneous — once you know, you're in the state of knowing. So Japanese uses 知っている ("I know" — the ongoing state) and 知らない ("I don't know" — notably not 知っていない). This asymmetry trips up learners: the positive is ている, but the negative is just ない. Accept it as a quirk and move on.

Particles: 知る takes を — the fact or information is your direct object. 名前を知っている (I know the name). Remember, you'll almost always use 知っている (the ongoing state) rather than the plain dictionary form.

Potential が知れる: grammatically valid but rarely used in conversation. 知れたことだ (it's an obvious thing) is a set phrase. For "can know," Japanese usually reaches for 分かる instead.

Passive に知られる: に marks who finds out. 秘密がみんなに知られた (the secret became known to everyone).

Causative に知らせる: に marks who you inform. This causative form is also a standalone word — 知らせる means "to notify, inform" and you'll encounter it independently: 結果を友達に知らせる (inform a friend of the result).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryしるを
Negativeしらないを
Politeしりますを
Polite negしりませんを
Pastしったを
Polite pastしりましたを
て-formしってを

Polite volしりましょうを
Passiveしられるに
Causativeしらせるに
Caus-passしらせられるに
Potentialしれるが
Imperativeしれを
Imp なさいしりなさいを

Cond ～たらしったらを

同じ is grammatically homeless. It behaves like a な-adjective sometimes (同じような), like a regular noun-modifier other times (同じ人 — note: no な), and occasionally even shows up with an い ending in its classical form 同じい. Textbooks usually file it under な-adjectives, but in practice 同じ goes directly before nouns with nothing in between. 同じ学校 (same school). 同じ時間 (same time). Just slam it in front of the noun. If it feels too easy, that's because it is.

The kanji 同 shows a mouth (口) inside a container (冂) with a single line across it. The idea: everything inside this boundary says the same thing. Mouths unified, speaking as one. It shows up in compounds where sameness or togetherness matters: 同時 (どうじ, simultaneous — same time), 同意 (どうい, agreement — same opinion), 共同 (きょうどう, cooperative — together-same).

One pattern worth locking in: 同じく is the adverb form — "likewise, similarly." And AとBは同じだ is how you say "A and B are the same." The particle と ("with") does the comparing. Not に, not が — と. This is one of those places where picking the wrong particle sounds immediately off.

いま stretches. When your friend texts 今どこ？ (where are you now?), that’s this second. But 今は忙しい (I’m busy now) could mean this hour, this week, this season of your life. And 今行く can mean “I’m going right this second” or “I’ll head out eventually” — it’s the speaker’s psychological now, not a precise timestamp. 今 fills whatever time window feels present to the person saying it.

今 is a compound machine. Bolt it onto other time words to anchor them to the present: 今日 (きょう, today), 今週 (こんしゅう, this week), 今月 (こんげつ, this month), 今年 (ことし, this year). Notice 今日 and 今年 have irregular readings — native Japanese words that got kanji slapped on them — while 今週 and 今月 use the Chinese-derived こん. You just have to memorize which is which.

The kanji itself: a lid (亜) clamping down on something below. A moment captured, pinned to the present. You can’t hold onto 今 — by the time you say it, it’s already slipping into the past — but the character tries anyway, pressing a lid on the instant to freeze it in place.

Here's a fun piece of linguistic history: 新しい is pronounced wrong. For over a thousand years, the correct reading was あらたしい — from the root あらた (new, fresh), which you still see in 新たに (あらたに, anew). Sometime during the Edo period, people started swapping the syllables — あらたしい became あたらしい — and the "mistake" stuck. Linguists call this metathesis, and it happens in English too ("comfortable" → "comftrable"). So every time you say あたらしい, you're perpetuating a 300-year-old pronunciation error that became the standard.

The kanji 新 is 立 (stand) + 木 (tree) + 斤 (axe). A standing tree being felled with an axe — the fresh-cut surface of wood, raw and new. The smell of cut timber. That sensory vividness is baked into the character.

新しい vs 新た: Both mean "new," but 新しい is everyday spoken Japanese while 新た (あらた) is literary and formal. 新しい靴 (new shoes) vs. 新たな発見 (a new discovery). You'll hear 新しい in conversation. You'll see 新たな in news articles and speeches.

Dictionaryあたらしい
Negativeあたらしくない
Pastあたらしかった
Past negあたらしくなかった
Adverbあたらしく
て-formあたらしくて

なる is one of the most fundamental verbs in the language — the verb of transformation. Things don't just "be" in Japanese the way they do in English. They become. Spring becomes summer. Children become adults. Silence becomes noise. なる is the engine behind all of it.

The particle it takes tells you everything. に marks the result — what something turns into. 医者になる (become a doctor). 大人になる (become an adult). With い-adjectives, you drop the い and add く: 暑くなる (become hot). With な-adjectives and nouns, に: 静かになる (become quiet). This pattern — [result] + になる or くなる — is how Japanese expresses all change of state. Master it and you unlock an enormous amount of the language.

なる vs する: These two are a philosophical pair. なる is change that happens — things become what they become, organically, without a visible hand. する is change that someone makes happen. 部屋がきれいになった (the room became clean — it just happened) vs. 部屋をきれいにした (I made the room clean — I did it). Japanese naturally gravitates toward なる. Where English says "I decided," Japanese often says 〜ことになった — "it became that..." — framing decisions as outcomes rather than acts of will.

Particles: なる takes に — the thing you become, the state you arrive at. に marks the destination of the transformation. 先生になる (become a teacher). With い-adjectives, the adjective itself changes form (〜くなる) so no particle is needed.

Passive になられる: rare and formal. You might hear it in honorific speech — 社長になられた (he became president — said respectfully). に marks what was become.

Causative を／にならせる: making someone become something. を for coercive, に for permissive. 息子を医者にならせる (make my son become a doctor). Uncommon — Japanese more naturally uses 〜にさせる with する instead.

Dictionaryなるに
Negativeならないに
Politeなりますに
Polite negなりませんに
Pastなったに
Polite pastなりましたに
て-formなってに
Volitionalなろうに
Polite volなりましょうに
Passiveなられるに
Causativeならせるを／に
Caus-passならせられるに
Potentialなれるに
Imperativeなれに
Imp なさいなりなさいに
Cond ～ばなればに
Cond ～たらなったらに

まだ lives a double life depending on whether the sentence is positive or negative. In a positive sentence, it means "still" — the situation continues: まだ雨が降っている (it's still raining). In a negative sentence, it means "not yet" — the situation hasn't started: まだ食べていない (I haven't eaten yet). Same word, opposite time directions. "Still" looks backward at something ongoing. "Not yet" looks forward at something expected.

The most useful phrase with まだ might be the one-word answer: まだ。 Someone asks if you're done? まだ。 (Not yet.) Are they here? まだ。 (Not yet.) Have you decided? まだ。 It works as a complete sentence because the negative verb is implied. The question already established the action — まだ just tells you it hasn't happened.

まだ vs もう: These are a pair and they contrast on two axes. もう食べた = already ate. まだ食べていない = haven't eaten yet. もう食べない = won't eat anymore. まだ食べている = still eating. Think of もう as the word for change (something shifted) and まだ as the word for no change (the old state persists). They push against each other in every conversation about time and completion.

あと points backward and forward at the same time. It means "after" in the sense of what comes next (あとで電話する I'll call later), but also "remaining" in the sense of what's left (あと三つ three more to go). The connecting idea: what trails behind. The work left behind to finish. The time left behind to pass.

The kanji, when written, is 後 — the same character used in 午後 (ごご, afternoon) and 後ろ (うしろ, behind). But in everyday writing, あと often appears in hiragana, especially in its casual, conversational uses. Written as 後 it feels more formal; as あと it's breezy and spoken.

The "remaining" sense is the one you'll use constantly. あと五分 (five more minutes). あと少し (just a little more). あと何がいる？ (what else do you need?). It's a countdown word — it tells you the gap between where you are and where you're headed. When someone says あとちょっと, they mean the finish line is close.

聞く does three jobs that English splits across three separate verbs: hear, listen, and ask. The logic is this: all three involve receiving information through your ears. You hear a sound. You listen to music. You ask a question — and then you hear the answer. The kanji 聞 makes this explicit: 耳 (ear) tucked inside 門 (gate). An ear at the gate, straining to catch what's being said on the other side.

Which meaning? Context and particles sort it out. 音楽を聞く (listen to music) — you're actively taking sound in. 先生に聞く (ask the teacher) — you're directing your ear toward a person for an answer. 声が聞こえる uses the related word 聞こえる (to be audible) for involuntary hearing — sounds that reach you whether you want them or not. 聞く itself implies at least some intention.

There's a separate kanji, 聴く, that means "listen" with deep attention — as in 音楽を聴く for focused, appreciative listening. The difference is like English "hear" vs "listen" cranked up a notch. In casual writing, 聞く covers everything. 聴く is for when you want to signal that you're really paying attention.

Particles: 聞く takes を for the thing heard or listened to — 音楽を聞く. For "ask," the person asked takes に — 先生に聞く (ask the teacher). The question content can be marked with を or framed with と: 道を聞く (ask for directions).

Potential が聞ける: が marks what can be heard. ここで生の音楽が聞ける (you can hear live music here). The thing becomes the subject — it's available to be heard.

Passive に聞かれる: に marks who overhears or asks you. 隣の人に聞かれた (the person next to me overheard). Often carries a nuance of unwanted exposure.

Causative に…を聞かせる: に marks the listener, を marks what they hear. 子供に音楽を聞かせる (have the kid listen to music). 聞かせる also works as a standalone meaning "to let someone hear" — いい話を聞かせてよ (tell me something good).

て-formきいてを

You will hear という in every third sentence of spoken Japanese. It means “called” or “that says” or “the fact that” and it’s built from this verb. 「桜」という花 (a flower called “sakura”). 行くということ (the fact that someone is going). 何という名前？ (what’s it called?). Once you start noticing という, it becomes impossible to un-notice — it’s the glue holding half of Japanese discourse together.

言う is the most basic speech verb. Not “speak” (that’s 話す), not “tell” exactly (that’s more 伝える) — just the act of producing words. 何と言った？ (what did you say?). The quotative particle と is its constant companion: 「行く」と言った (said “I’m going”). Almost every use of 言う has と right before it, framing the words that were said.

The kanji 言 is ancient and blunt: a mouth (口) with lines coming out of it. Sound waves, words, speech made visible. Every kanji containing 言 as a radical relates to language: 話 (speak), 語 (language), 読 (read), 記 (record). When you see 言 or its compressed form 訁 on the left side of a character, words are involved.

Particles: 言う takes と to frame what's said — 「はい」と言う. The person you say it to takes に — 友達に言う (say to a friend). Content can also take を — 文句を言う (say a complaint / complain).

Potential が言える: が marks what can be said. 日本語で名前が言える (I can say my name in Japanese).

Passive に言われる: に marks who said it. 先生にそう言われた (the teacher told me that). Very common — being told things by others is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を言わせる: に marks who you make speak, を marks what they say. 子供に「ごめんなさい」を言わせる (make the child say sorry). Also used idiomatically: 言わせてもらえば (if you'll let me say...).

Imp なさいいいなさいと
Cond ～ばいえばと

少ない is 多い's twin — and it shares the same grammatical curse. Just like 多い, you can't put 少ない directly before a noun. 少ない人 doesn't work. You need 少ない人数 or rephrase entirely: 人が少ない (people are few). These are the only two い-adjectives in the language with this restriction. Nobody has a satisfying explanation for why. They're just the rebels.

The kanji 少 is 小 (small) with an extra stroke — a slash cutting through it, as if paring something down even further. Small, then smaller. Not much left. The two readings split neatly: すくない as an adjective (few), and すこし as a noun/adverb (a little bit). Same kanji, two words. 時間が少ない (there's little time — adjective). 少し待って (wait a bit — adverb).

少ない vs 少し: Learners sometimes confuse these. 少ない describes a quantity that's insufficient or notably small — there's a judgment in it. 少し is neutral — just "a small amount." 経験が少ない means you have too little experience. 少し経験がある means you have a bit of experience. One implies a problem. The other doesn't.

Dictionaryすくない
Negativeすくなくない
Pastすくなかった
Past negすくなくなかった
Adverbすくなく
て-formすくなくて

高い means two things that seem unrelated in English — "tall" and "expensive" — but in Japanese the connection is obvious. Both point upward. A tall building reaches high. A high price is an elevated number. The kanji 高 is a picture of a tall building: a base, walls, and a peaked roof. The oldest forms looked like a watchtower or a multi-story structure. Height, in every sense.

Which meaning applies is always clear from context. 背が高い (tall — height of a person). 値段が高い (expensive — the price is high). 山が高い (the mountain is tall). この店は高い (this shop is expensive). You'll also hear it for figurative height: レベルが高い (high level), 評価が高い (highly rated).

Opposite pair: For height, the opposite is 低い (ひくい, low/short). For price, the opposite is 安い (やすい, cheap). Japanese doesn't mind that one adjective has two different antonyms — each meaning lives in its own lane.

Dictionaryたかい
Negativeたかくない
Pastたかかった
Past negたかくなかった
Adverbたかく
て-formたかくて

A small controversy hides inside this word. 供 originally meant “attendant” or “one who serves” — making 子供 literally “child-attendant,” children seen as little people who accompany adults. Some modern writers find that connotation uncomfortable and write 子ども instead, using hiragana to soften it. Government documents have gone back and forth. You’ll see both spellings, and which one someone uses can signal their stance on linguistic politics.

The kanji 子 alone is a drawing of a baby — the oldest forms showed a swaddled infant with arms outstretched. As a suffix, it turns up in girls’ names (花子, 洋子), in words for small things (椅子 chair, 帽子 hat), and in scientific terms (原子 atom, 電子 electron).

子供 is the general, everyday word for “child/children.” No singular/plural distinction. For your own child: うちの子 (my kid — using the intimate うち). For someone else’s child politely: お子さん (your child — with the honorific お and さん). The register shift is automatic: your own children are 子供, other people’s are お子さん.

そう belongs to the ko-so-a-do system — it's the そ member, which means it refers to something near the listener or something just mentioned. そうです (that's right / that's so) is agreement — you're confirming what the other person just said. Their statement is in "their zone," so it gets そう.

This tiny word handles an enormous amount of conversational work. そうだ after a plain form means hearsay — "I heard that..." 雨が降るそうだ (I hear it's going to rain). そう after a verb stem means "looks like" — 降りそう (looks like it'll rain). Same sound, completely different grammar, and you tell them apart by what comes before そう. These are separate grammatical constructions that happen to use the same word — don't try to unify them. Just learn them as distinct patterns.

In conversation, そう is the universal back-channel response. Someone tells you something and you need to acknowledge it? そう。 そうですか。 そうなんだ。 Each has a slightly different flavor — from flat acknowledgment to mild surprise to genuine "oh, really?" — but they all run on the same そう engine.

もう is the word of change. Something shifted. The old state ended and a new one began — or is about to. That's the thread connecting all its seemingly different meanings: "already" (the change happened), "soon" (the change is imminent), "anymore" (in negative: the change means it's over), and even "another" (one more — adding to the count).

In positive sentences: もう食べた (already ate — it's done). もう帰る (I'm heading home now — the moment of departure has arrived). もうすぐ (soon — the change is close). In negative sentences: もう食べない (won't eat anymore — that's finished). もう会えない (can't meet anymore). The positive and negative uses feel opposite but the core is identical: a transition point has been reached.

Then there's the counting sense, which seems unrelated: もう一つ (one more). もう一回 (one more time). Here もう means "additionally" — you've already got some, and you're adding to the pile. It's still about movement past a previous state: you had one, now you have another.

And then there's the exclamation: もう！ — frustration, exasperation, "ugh, enough already!" It's もう at its most emotional — a declaration that you've hit your limit.

Call a Japanese middle schooler a 学生 and someone will mentally correct you. Japanese has separate words for students at different levels. 学生 (がくせい) means a university student — that’s its default lane. 生徒 (せいと) is for middle and high school students. 児童 (じどう) is for elementary school kids. In casual speech 学生 can stretch to cover students in general, but in formal contexts, the distinctions matter.

The kanji: 学 (study) + 生 (life, person) = a person whose life is studying. 学 is worth examining: the top part (⺍) represents hands reaching for knowledge, the middle (冖) is a roof, and the bottom is 子 (a child). A child under a roof, hands grasping upward.

学生 also functions as an identity marker beyond academics. 学生割引 (がくせいわりびき, student discount). 学生時代 (がくせいじだい, one’s student days) — a nostalgic reference to a specific chapter of life with its own rules, expectations, and freedoms.

Japanese has two words pronounced あつい that both mean "hot" — and mixing them up is one of the classic beginner errors. 熱い is hot to the touch: hot coffee, a hot stove, a feverish forehead. 暑い is hot weather: a sweltering summer day, a stuffy room. The distinction is physical: 熱い is about an object's temperature. 暑い is about the air around you.

The kanji tells the story. 熱 has the fire radical (灬, four dots representing flames) at the bottom — fire heating something up, making an object hot. 暑 has 日 (sun) at the top — the sun beating down, making the environment hot. Fire from below vs. sun from above. Object heat vs. ambient heat.

熱い also carries a figurative meaning: passionate, intense. 熱い男 (a passionate man — someone who burns with intensity). 熱い試合 (an intense match). The metaphor is the same as English "burning passion" — but in Japanese it uses the touch-heat word, not the weather-heat word. Passion is something you feel radiating from a person, like heat from a surface.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

どうぞ is the gesture of an open hand — "here, take it," "go ahead," "after you." It's not the requesting kind of "please" (that's ください). It's the offering kind. You use it when you're giving permission, presenting something, or stepping aside for someone else.

どうぞ座ってください (please, have a seat). どうぞお入りください (please come in). どうぞ alone, with a gesture — handing someone a drink, holding a door open, offering a chair. The word carries warmth. It says: I want you to have this, do this, be comfortable. It's hospitality compressed into three syllables.

どうぞ vs ぜひ: Both get translated as "please" in some contexts, but they're different. どうぞ is offering or permitting — the ball is in the other person's court. ぜひ (by all means) is enthusiasm — you're urging someone to do something because you think they should: ぜひ来てください (you should definitely come). どうぞ opens a door. ぜひ pushes you through it.

午後 literally means “after noon” — and English “afternoon” is the exact same construction. Two languages, same logic, built independently. 午 means “noon” and 後 means “after.” Its pair is 午前 (ごぜん, before noon / AM). Together they split the day at its midpoint.

The zodiac connection goes deeper. 午 is the seventh of the twelve earthly branches, assigned to the horse (馬). That’s why “exactly noon” in Japanese is 正午 (しょうご) — the precise middle of the horse hour. And the cardinal direction south is also associated with 午, because the sun is due south at noon. 子午線 (しごせん, the meridian line) literally runs from 子 (rat/north/midnight) to 午 (horse/south/noon).

In practice, 午後 goes before the time: 午後三時 (3 PM). In conversation, people more often just use context or the 24-hour clock. 午後 is the formal, precise marker — schedules, appointments, news broadcasts.

社長, 校長, 部長 — company president, school principal, department head. All end in 長. What does any of that have to do with “long”? Everything. The kanji started as a picture of a person with very long hair streaming past their waist. Long hair was a mark of old age and wisdom in ancient China. The person with the longest hair has been around the longest — so 長 means both “long” and “chief.”

長い covers length in space and time. 長い道 (a long road). 長い時間 (a long time). 話が長い (the talk is long — someone won’t stop talking). Unlike English, where “long” and “tall” split, Japanese uses 長い for horizontal extension and 高い for vertical — a rope is 長い, a building is 高い.

Its opposite, 短い (みじかい, short), forms one of the cleanest antonym pairs in Japanese.

Dictionaryながい
Negativeながくない
Pastながかった
Past negながくなかった
Adverbながく
て-formながくて

本 doesn’t mean “book.” Not originally. It means “root, origin, the real thing.” The kanji is 木 (tree) with a horizontal line crossing the base of the trunk — marking the root, the foundation. Books came later, because books were considered the root of knowledge. The “origin” meaning lives on everywhere: 日本 (にほん, Japan — “origin of the sun”), 本当 (ほんとう, true — “original reality”), 本人 (ほんにん, the person themselves), 基本 (きほん, fundamentals).

As a counter, 本 counts long, thin objects: pencils, bottles, roads, rivers, movies (reels of film were long), phone calls (running through wires). ビール三本 (three bottles of beer). 映画を一本見た (watched one movie). The counter readings shift: いっぽん, にほん, さんぼん...

For “book” specifically, 本 is the everyday word. 本を読む (read a book). 本屋 (ほんや, bookstore). 絵本 (えほん, picture book).

The reading is the interesting part. 今 is normally いま or こん. 年 is normally ねん or とし. But 今年 is neither いまねん nor こんねん — it's ことし, a native Japanese word that got the Chinese characters pasted over it. こ is an archaic prefix meaning "this" (same こ as in ここ, これ) and とし is the native word for "year." The reading predates the kanji — it's ancient Japanese wearing a Chinese-character costume.

This is part of a family: 今日 (きょう, today), 今朝 (けさ, this morning), 今年 (ことし, this year) — all have irregular readings because they're old native words. Meanwhile, 今週 (こんしゅう, this week) and 今月 (こんげつ, this month) use regular Chinese-derived readings. The rule of thumb: the more frequently a time word is used in daily speech, the more likely it is to have kept its native Japanese reading.

今年こそ is a phrase you'll hear every January — "this year, for sure." The こそ adds emphasis, making it a declaration. New Year's resolutions, Japanese style.

よく is the adverb form of いい/よい (good) — and it kept both senses when it became an adverb. "Well" is straightforward: よくできた (well done). よく分かる (I understand well). But "often"? That seems like a leap — until you realize the logic. Doing something well implies doing it thoroughly, which implies doing it frequently. You know a place well because you go there often. The meanings bleed into each other.

Context sorts it out instantly. よく食べるね (you eat a lot / you eat often). よく聞こえる (I can hear well). よく来たね (good of you to come / I'm glad you came) — this one plays on both meanings at once.

There's also a sarcastic/exasperated よく: よくそんなことが言えるね (how can you say such a thing?). Here it means something like "you have the nerve to" — it's marveling at someone's audacity. The "well" meaning twisted into "how dare you do that so confidently." This usage is common in arguments and drama.

彼女 has a split personality. In formal or written Japanese, it means "she" — a third-person pronoun. In casual speech, it means "girlfriend." 彼女は来ますか in a business context means "is she coming?" At a bar with friends, 彼女いるの？ means "do you have a girlfriend?" The same word, but context flips the meaning entirely.

The kanji: 彼 (that person, he) + 女 (woman). Literally, "that woman." 彼 alone means "he" or "boyfriend" (same dual-meaning pattern). These words were actually coined in the Meiji era — Japan needed pronouns to translate Western novels, so scholars repurposed old demonstrative words. Before that, Japanese didn't really have third-person pronouns the way European languages do. People were referred to by name, title, or role.

This is important: native Japanese speakers use 彼女 and 彼 far less than English speakers use "she" and "he." In conversation, you refer to people by name — 田中さんは..., not 彼女は.... Overusing 彼女/彼 as pronouns is a tell that someone learned Japanese through translation rather than conversation. Use them when the context genuinely calls for them, not as default substitutes for people's names.

どう is the question member of the ko-so-a-do manner series: こう (like this), そう (like that), ああ (like that over there), どう (how?). It asks about the manner or state of something — not "what" (that's 何) but "in what way."

The phrase you'll use most: どうですか (how is it?). It's the Swiss army knife of Japanese questions. Asking about food at a restaurant? どうですか。 Someone's health? どうですか。 A proposal? どうですか。 It works for anything where you want someone's impression or assessment.

どう also appears in compounds that are so common they feel like single words. どうして (why — literally "doing what way"). どうも (thanks, somehow, very much — a vague intensifier that defies clean translation). どうぞ (please, go ahead). どうしても (no matter what, by all means). Master these どう compounds and you've unlocked a huge chunk of everyday conversational Japanese.

Words are leaves. That’s the metaphor baked into 言葉: 言 (say, speech) + 葉 (leaf). They grow from branches of thought and scatter in the wind. Some scholars think the image comes from words multiplying like leaves on a tree. Others point to the 万葉集 (まんようしゅう, Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), Japan’s oldest poetry anthology. Either way, it’s one of the most beautiful kanji compounds in the language.

ことば is broader than English “word.” A single word is a ことば. A phrase is a ことば. An entire language is ことば. Even the way someone speaks — their manner of expression — is their ことば. 言葉が見つからない (I can’t find the words). 言葉遣い (ことばづかい, how someone talks).

A Japanese proverb: 言葉は心の使い — “words are the messengers of the heart.” In a culture that values reading between the lines and understanding what’s not said, 言葉 carries extra weight.

顔が広い means “having a wide face.” It’s a compliment — you know lots of people, you’re well-connected. 顔を出す means “to show one’s face” — making an appearance. 顔を立てる means “to preserve someone’s face” — protecting their dignity. 顔が利く means “one’s face works” — having influence. In Japanese, 顔 isn’t just anatomy. It’s identity, reputation, and social standing in a single word.

The kanji: 頁 (head) on the right — that component shows up in every head-related kanji (頭 head, 額 forehead, 顎 jaw). The left side, 彦, was originally a man of fine appearance. Together: the part of the head that shows your character.

In daily use, 顔 handles everything from 顔を洗う (wash your face) to 見覚えのある顔 (a familiar face). For the physical face in a medical sense, Japanese uses 顔面 (がんめん). But in conversation, 顔 carries the full weight of both the physical and the social.

〜終わる attached to a verb’s masu-stem means “finish doing.” 読み終わる (finish reading). 食べ終わる (finish eating). This compound verb pattern tells you not just that something ended, but that someone completed an action to its conclusion. Learn it first — you’ll reach for it constantly.

On its own, 終わる is intransitive — things end by themselves. 授業が終わる (class ends). 映画が終わった (the movie ended). Its transitive partner is 終える (おえる) — you actively finish something: 仕事を終える (finish the work).

The kanji 終 is 糸 (thread) + 冬 (winter). The end of the thread. Winter — the end of the growing season.

Particles: 終わる takes が — the thing that ends is the subject. 会議が終わる (the meeting ends). Since it's intransitive, there's no direct object — things end by themselves.

Passive に終わられる: rare, but used for indirect passive — when something ending negatively affects you. 先に終わられた (they finished before me — and it put me at a disadvantage).

Causative を／に終わらせる: making something end. を marks what you're ending — 会議を終わらせる (end the meeting). This causative form essentially works like the transitive version.

Japanese has two number systems running in parallel. The Chinese-derived system (いち、に、さん...) and the native Japanese system (ひとつ、ふたつ、みっつ...). ひとつ is the native system's "one" — and it's the system you use when counting generic things without a specific counter. Don't know what counter to use? ひとつ works. It's the safe default.

The native counting system only goes to ten (とお) and then stops. After that, everything switches to Chinese numbers. This tells you something about its age — it predates extensive Chinese linguistic influence, and it was probably sufficient for daily life in an era before large-scale accounting. You'll encounter all ten (ひとつ through とお) regularly, so they're worth memorizing as a set.

一つ also works as "one" in the sense of an option or a point: 一つ聞いてもいい？ (can I ask you one thing?). 一つ問題がある (there's one problem). And it shows up in the expression 一つずつ (one by one), where ずつ means "each" — a useful pattern that works with any number.

Japanese has three verbs for "give" and they're not interchangeable — they encode the social direction of the gift. あげる means giving away from the speaker: I give to you, I give to someone, they give to someone. くれる means giving toward the speaker: you give to me, someone gives to me. もらう means receiving. Get these mixed up and your sentence will sound like the social gravity is backwards.

あげる literally means "to raise" — the same word as lifting something up, 手を上げる (raise your hand). The giving sense may come from the gesture of raising something up to offer it to someone. Or from the idea of elevating — putting the other person above you. Either way, あげる inherently positions the receiver as higher.

Key pattern: 〜てあげる means doing something as a favor for someone. 教えてあげる (I'll teach you — as a favor). 手伝ってあげた (I helped them — implying generosity). Be careful: this pattern can sound condescending if used wrong. 説明してあげる can come across as "I'll graciously explain it to you" if the context doesn't warrant it. Use it when the favor is real.

Particles: あげる takes に for the receiver and を for the thing given. 友達にプレゼントをあげる (give a present to a friend). に marks the destination — where the gift ends up.

Potential があげられる: が marks what can be given. いいアドバイスがあげられる (I can give good advice).

Passive にあげられる: rarely used — giving is inherently active, not something done to you. You'd more naturally use もらう to express the receiving side.

Causative にあげさせる: に marks who you make give. 子供にお菓子をあげさせる (make the child give out candy).

こう is the こ member of the manner series: こう (like this — my way), そう (like that — your way), ああ (like that — distant), どう (how?). Where これ points at a thing near you, こう points at a manner or method near you — something you're about to demonstrate or have just shown.

It almost always comes with a demonstration. こうやって開ける (you open it like this) — and then you show them. こう書く (you write it like this) — and you write it. こう is the word you say while your hands are moving, while you're physically showing someone the way to do something. It's deictic — it points at something present in the shared moment.

In storytelling, こう introduces direct speech or thought: 彼はこう言った (he said the following). It sets up what's about to come — "here's what it was like." And in arguments or reasoning: こう考えてみて (think about it this way) — you're about to reframe the situation from your angle.

Pronunciation trap: がっこう, not がくこう. The く of 学 gets swallowed into a double consonant before the こ of 校. This sound change (促音, そくおん) happens regularly when certain syllables bump up against voiceless consonants — 六階 → ろっかい, 一回 → いっかい. Recognize this pattern early and you’ll avoid mispronouncing dozens of compounds.

学 (study) + 校 (school building). The second kanji has the tree radical (木) and 交 (cross, exchange). The original meaning was a wooden frame for binding or checking things — which evolved into both “a place of learning” and “to proofread” (校正, こうせい).

学校 is the general word for school (elementary through high school). For university: 大学. For specific types: 小学校 (しょうがっこう, elementary), 中学校 (ちゅうがっこう, middle school), 高校 (こうこう, high school — shortened from 高等学校).

くれる is giving seen from the receiver's side — and specifically, the receiver is you (or your in-group). This is the verb that makes Japanese giving/receiving unique: the language forces you to encode who benefits. あげる sends gifts outward. くれる receives them inward. 友達がプレゼントをくれた (my friend gave me a present). The friend is the subject, but the emotional center is the speaker — someone did something nice for me.

The crucial pattern: 〜てくれる means someone does something as a favor for you. 教えてくれた (they taught me — and I'm grateful). 手伝ってくれる？ (will you help me?). This is warmer and more personal than a neutral request. Using 〜てくれる acknowledges that the other person's action benefits you — there's built-in gratitude. Compare 手伝ってもらえる？ (more about the outcome) with 手伝ってくれる？ (more about the person helping). Both are requests, but くれる feels more direct and personal.

くれる vs もらう: Both describe someone doing you a favor, but from different angles. 友達が教えてくれた (friend taught me — friend is the subject, friend is generous). 友達に教えてもらった (I got taught by my friend — I am the subject, I received the benefit). Same event, different framing. Japanese uses both constantly.

Particles: くれる takes を for the thing given. The giver is the subject (marked by が). 母が本をくれた (mom gave me a book). The receiver (you) is usually omitted because it's implied.

Potential がくれられる: almost never used. If you want to say someone "can give," rephrase with もらえる — "I can receive."

Passive: not used — くれる already encodes the receiver's perspective, making passive redundant.

Causative: not naturally used. The giving/receiving system doesn't layer with causative in practice.
